serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Fish Black Pepper is approximately 250 g, typically served as a main dish alongside rice or flatbread in a Singaporean Indian meal.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Fish, mixed species, cooked, dry heat (SR Legacy ID 15264) and spice composition data; adapted with ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 for spiced fish dish estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / ICMR-National Institute of Nutrition, India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for 'Fish Black Pepper' exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked fish (dry heat) as the protein/fat/energy base, with carbohydrate, sodium, and micronutrient adjustments informed by ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 for spiced fish preparations. Input values from the source record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are retained where they align with the adapted source range. Energy 168 kcal, protein 14 g, fat 10 g, carbohydrate 6 g per 100 g are consistent with a pan-fried spiced fish dish with masala gravy. Values are accepted at medium confidence.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — analogous estimate based on cooked fish with spices (low-carbohydrate protein-dominant dish)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Fish Black Pepper. GI estimated at 25 (low) based on analogous low-carbohydrate cooked fish dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database and the International Tables of GI Values (Atkinson et al., 2008). The dish is protein-dominant (14 g/100 g) with only 6 g/100 g carbohydrate from onion, tomato, and spices — all low-GI ingredients. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (25 × 6.0 × 2.5) / 100 = 3.75, rounded to 3.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 340 mg/100 g (850 mg per 250 g serving) approaches 37% of the 2,300 mg daily limit in a single serving.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and monitor total daily sodium intake carefully, as a full 250 g serving contributes approximately 850 mg sodium; pairing with potassium-rich vegetables such as tomatoes and spinach can partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (6 g/100 g) and high protein (14 g/100 g) result in a low glycemic load of approximately 3.8 per serving, with minimal post-meal glucose impact.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fish Black Pepper is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low glycemic load and high protein content; it should be paired with low-GI carbohydrate sides such as brown rice or cauliflower rice rather than large portions of white rice to maintain glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is moderate at 2.8 g/100 g (7 g per serving); sodium at 340 mg/100 g requires monitoring; however, fish provides lean protein and potential omega-3 fatty acids depending on species.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease may consume Fish Black Pepper in moderate portions, choosing omega-3-rich fish species such as kingfish or pomfret, requesting minimal cooking oil, and limiting serving frequency to manage cumulative saturated fat and sodium intake within daily cardiovascular dietary targets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spice profile including turmeric (curcumin), black pepper (piperine), ginger, and garlic; piperine enhances curcumin bioavailability.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Fish Black Pepper is beneficial for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its anti-inflammatory spice combination and lean fish protein; nightshade-sensitive individuals should monitor their response to tomatoes and green chillies and may request reduced quantities.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fish Black Pepper is a Singaporean Indian dish consisting of fish pieces — commonly kingfish, pomfret, or basa — pan-fried or stir-fried and cooked in a bold black pepper-based masala. The preparation typically involves marinating the fish in turmeric, salt, and spices before searing, then finishing in a dry or semi-gravy sauce made from onions, tomatoes, garlic, ginger, black pepper, curry leaves, and coriander. The result is a savoury, aromatic dish with a sharp peppery heat, tender fish texture, and a rich spice-forward flavour profile. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 168 kcal, 14 g protein, 10 g fat (2.8 g saturated), and only 6 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate option with a low glycemic index of approximately 25. The dish is halal and gluten-free. It is clinically suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its low glycemic load (approximately 3.8 per 250 g serving) and high protein content. Sodium at 340 mg/100 g warrants monitoring for hypertension. The anti-inflammatory spice combination — particularly turmeric and black pepper — provides curcumin-piperine synergy relevant to inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis. Commonly served as a main course alongside steamed rice, roti, or flatbread in Singaporean Indian restaurants and hawker stalls.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Fish Black Pepper exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, USDA FoodData Central). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked fish (dry heat) and ICMR-NIN spiced fish dish data. The input record was ai_generated with confidence 0.7; values are plausible and consistent with the adapted source range but cannot be verified against a direct measurement. Medium confidence is appropriate.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, full micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific spiced fish preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 at the dish level as of 2024. Calcium (20 mg), iron (1.0 mg), cholesterol (40 mg), and vitamin C (6 mg) are retained from the input record as plausible estimates consistent with cooked fish and spice composition data. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate cooked fish dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Fish Black Pepper exists in published literature. The ethnic_tag 'Naan' in the original input has been removed as it is not a characteristic ingredient or tag for this dish. Trans fat at 0.04 g/100 g is a trace value from cooking oil and is retained as reported.
